reasoning: ACLED data indicates robust and continuously active conflict in South Sudan throughout 2024 and 2025, with frequent violent events (e.g., a major spike in late 2025 noted to be the highest since mid-2016). Because the historical baseline is already quite high (averaging ~87 to 150+ true fatalities per month), a 10x spike would require an absolutely massive escalation, equivalent to 1,000 to 1,500+ fatalities in a single 30-day window. Empirically, it is extremely rare for a country with high endemic violence to experience a 10x spike over its 12-month moving average; such multipliers typically only happen when peaceful countries suddenly descend into war. The reference value of "1.000" noted for December 2025 is highly likely a data lag artifact (reflecting exactly 0 recent deaths prior to a data update) or a formatted representation of 1,000. Either way, as ACLED updates and backfills its datasets upon resolution, the accurate 360-day average will normalize to a high baseline, making a mathematically 10x larger outcome structurally improbable for both the January and February 2026 windows. I assign slightly higher uncertainty to the latter date.
